facts.claim: Most foreign nationals, including EU, US, and UK passport holders, require a visa to enter Tanzania (Zanzibar), obtainable via e-visa online or upon arrival. A single-entry tourist visa for EU/UK citizens costs around $50 USD and is valid for up to 90 days, while US citizens can get a multiple-entry visa for $100 USD valid for one year.

sources.name: Tanzania Immigration Department
sources.quote: Most foreign nationals require a visa to enter Tanzania, which includes Zanzibar. Travelers can typically obtain a visa through an e-visa system online or upon arrival at designated entry points.

sources.name: VisaHQ
sources.quote: EU citizens need a visa and are eligible to apply for a Tanzania e-visa online. A single-entry tourist visa typically costs around $50 USD and is valid for up to 90 days.

sources.name: U.S. Department of State
sources.quote: US citizens require a visa. They can apply for an e-visa online or obtain a visa on arrival. A multiple-entry visa for US citizens costs $100 USD and is valid for one year.

sources.name: Original Travel
sources.quote: British citizens need a tourist or business visa. They can apply for an e-visa online or get a single-entry tourist visa upon arrival at main ports of entry. The single-entry tourist visa costs approximately $50 USD and is valid for up to 90 days.

facts.claim: A yellow fever vaccination certificate is mandatory for travelers aged one year or older arriving from or transiting for more than 12 hours through a country with a risk of yellow fever transmission. Additionally, there is a high risk of malaria in all areas of Tanzania below 1,800m, including Zanzibar, making malaria prophylaxis recommended.

sources.name: Travel Health Pro
sources.quote: A yellow fever vaccination certificate is required for travelers aged one year or older arriving from, or having transited for more than 12 hours through, a country with a risk of yellow fever transmission.

sources.name: Passport Health USA
sources.quote: There is a high risk of malaria in all areas of Tanzania below 1,800m, including Zanzibar. It is recommended that visitors consult their doctor about malaria prophylaxis, with common medications including atovaquone/proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine.

facts.claim: As of October 1, 2024, all visitors to Zanzibar (excluding residents) must have mandatory Inbound Travel Insurance from the Zanzibar Insurance Corporation (ZIC) for the duration of their stay (up to 92 days).

sources.name: Tanzania Tourism Board
sources.quote: As of October 1, 2024, all visitors to Zanzibar (excluding residents) must have mandatory Inbound Travel Insurance from the Zanzibar Insurance Corporation (ZIC) for the duration of their stay (up to 92 days).

facts.claim: The long dry season (June to October) is considered the best time to visit Zanzibar for minimal rainfall, clear skies, and comfortable temperatures, ideal for beaches, snorkeling, and diving, also coinciding with the safari high season. The short dry season (December to February) is also popular with warm, bright, and sunny weather.

sources.name: Zanzibar.com
sources.quote: Long Dry Season (June to October): This is considered the best time to visit, with minimal rainfall, clear skies, and comfortable temperatures, ideal for beaches, snorkeling, and diving. This period also coincides with the safari high season on mainland Tanzania.

sources.name: Go2Africa
sources.quote: Short Dry Season (December to February): Another popular period with warm, bright, and sunny weather, excellent for beach holidays and diving.

facts.claim: Excellent visibility and calm seas for diving and snorkeling are typically found from September to March, with whale shark encounters common from October to February/December around Mafia Island and Mnemba Atoll.

sources.name: Fun Divers Zanzibar
sources.quote: Excellent visibility and calm seas are typically found from September to March. Whale shark encounters are common from October to February/December, especially around Mafia Island and Mnemba Atoll.

sources.name: Girls That Scuba
sources.quote: Excellent visibility and calm seas are typically found from September to March. Whale shark encounters are common from October to February/December, especially around Mafia Island and Mnemba Atoll.

facts.claim: Abeid Amani Karume International Airport (ZNZ) is Zanzibar's primary international gateway, located 5-6 kilometers south of Zanzibar City (Stone Town). Direct flights are available from European cities like Frankfurt (Condor, Lufthansa) and Amsterdam (KLM).

sources.name: Skyscanner
sources.quote: The primary international gateway to Zanzibar is Abeid Amani Karume International Airport (ZNZ), located on Unguja Island, approximately 5-6 kilometers south of Zanzibar City (Stone Town).

sources.name: Condor Airlines
sources.quote: Direct flights are available from cities like Istanbul (Turkish Airlines), Frankfurt (Condor, Lufthansa), Amsterdam (KLM), and Rome (Neos).

sources.name: Lufthansa
sources.quote: Direct flights are available from cities like Istanbul (Turkish Airlines), Frankfurt (Condor, Lufthansa), Amsterdam (KLM), and Rome (Neos).

sources.name: KLM Royal Dutch Airlines
sources.quote: Direct flights are available from cities like Istanbul (Turkish Airlines), Frankfurt (Condor, Lufthansa), Amsterdam (KLM), and Rome (Neos).

facts.claim: Abeid Amani Karume International Airport (ZNZ) is about 5-6 kilometers south of Zanzibar City (Stone Town), and taxis, private transfers, or pre-arranged lodge transfers are readily available to Stone Town and other destinations.

sources.name: Zanzibar.com
sources.quote: Abeid Amani Karume International Airport (ZNZ) is situated about 5-6 kilometers south of Zanzibar City (Stone Town).

sources.name: Original Travel
sources.quote: Taxis and private transfers are readily available from the airport to Stone Town and other destinations across the island. Many luxury lodges and resorts offer pre-arranged transfers for their guests.

facts.claim: Whale sharks aggregate at Mafia Island, Tanzania, from October to February each year, with peak sightings in November and December due to warm, plankton-rich waters.

sources.name: WWF
sources.quote: Best Season: October to February each year.

sources.name: Sea Sense
sources.quote: Best Season: October to February each year.

sources.name: African Wild Trekkers
sources.quote: Peak Months: November and December are considered the most reliable for sightings. October, November, and December offer the highest concentration due to warm, plankton-rich waters.

sources.name: Serengeti Clarity
sources.quote: Peak Months: November and December are considered the most reliable for sightings. October, November, and December offer the highest concentration due to warm, plankton-rich waters.

facts.claim: The global whale shark population is estimated at approximately 130,000 to 200,000 individuals, having decreased by about 50% over the last 75 years, with Indo-Pacific populations declining by approximately 63%.

sources.name: Wikipedia
sources.quote: Estimate: Approximately 130,000 to 200,000 individuals globally, with numbers declining. Trend: The global whale shark population has decreased by about 50% over the last 75 years. Indo-Pacific populations have seen a more dramatic decline of approximately 63%.

sources.name: IFAW
sources.quote: Estimate: Approximately 130,000 to 200,000 individuals globally, with numbers declining. Trend: The global whale shark population has decreased by about 50% over the last 75 years. Indo-Pacific populations have seen a more dramatic decline of approximately 63%.

facts.claim: In Mafia Island, Tanzania, whale shark sightings are high during peak season, with 5 to 30 individuals simultaneously visible at the peak of the October and November season, and up to 8 sightings recorded in a day.

sources.name: WWF
sources.quote: Probability: High during peak season, with whale sharks easily seen nearshore. Up to 8 sightings can be recorded in a day by viewing or swimming. Concentration: 5 to 30 individuals can be simultaneously visible at the peak of the October and November season, exceeding most other Indian Ocean sites.

sources.name: African Wild Trekkers
sources.quote: Probability: High during peak season, with whale sharks easily seen nearshore. Up to 8 sightings can be recorded in a day by viewing or swimming. Concentration: 5 to 30 individuals can be simultaneously visible at the peak of the October and November season, exceeding most other Indian Ocean sites.

facts.claim: Tanzania's marine wildlife and protected areas are managed by the Tanzania Marine Parks and Reserves Unit (MPRU), established under the Marine Parks and Reserves Act No. 29 of 1994, operating under the Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries.

sources.name: Tanzania Marine Parks and Reserves Unit
sources.quote: Official Authority: Tanzania Marine Parks and Reserves Unit (MPRU), established under the Marine Parks and Reserves Act No. 29 of 1994, operating under the Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries.

sources.quote: Official Authority: Tanzania Marine Parks and Reserves Unit (MPRU), established under the Marine Parks and Reserves Act No. 29 of 1994, operating under the Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries.

sources.name: TZ Online
sources.quote: Official Authority: Tanzania Marine Parks and Reserves Unit (MPRU), established under the Marine Parks and Reserves Act No. 29 of 1994, operating under the Ministry of Livestock and Fisheries.

facts.claim: Sea Sense conducts education workshops for fishers and training workshops for whale shark tourism boat captains and crew in Mafia, Tanzania, focusing on whale shark biology, behavior, boat handling, and visitor management to reduce negative encounters.

sources.name: Sea Sense
sources.quote: Guide Compliance/Training: Sea Sense conducts education workshops for fishers and training workshops for whale shark tourism boat captains and crew in Mafia. These workshops focus on whale shark biology, behavior, boat handling, and visitor management to reduce negative encounters. Codes of Conduct: Sea Sense, in collaboration with the Marine Megafauna Foundation, produced a 'Whale Shark Briefing Pack' that includes a 'Code of Conduct' for visitors.

sources.name: Marine Megafauna Foundation
sources.quote: Codes of Conduct: Sea Sense, in collaboration with the Marine Megafauna Foundation, produced a 'Whale Shark Briefing Pack' that includes a 'Code of Conduct' for visitors.

sources.quote: Current IUCN Red List Status: Endangered. Population Trend: Decreasing, with a global decline of over 50% in the last 75 years. Last Assessment Year: Listed as Endangered in July 2016.

facts.claim: The Green Sea Turtle (Chelonia mydas) has a global IUCN Red List status of 'Least Concern' (reclassified in 2025), with an improved/increasing global trend, though subpopulations vary: North Indian Ocean is Vulnerable (2019), Southwest Indian Ocean is Least Concern (2023), and East Indian-West Pacific is Least Concern.

sources.name: IUCN-SSC Marine Turtle Specialist Group
sources.quote: Global Current IUCN Red List Status: Least Concern. (Previously Endangered since 1982). Population Trend: Globally improved/increasing trend, but populations remain below historical numbers. Last Assessment Year: Reclassified to 'Least Concern' in 2025. Subpopulations in Indian Ocean: North Indian Ocean Subpopulation: Vulnerable (last assessed 2019). Southwest Indian Ocean Subpopulation: Least Concern (last assessed 2023). East Indian-West Pacific Subpopulation: Least Concern.

sources.name: CMS
sources.quote: Global Current IUCN Red List Status: Least Concern. (Previously Endangered since 1982). East Indian-West Pacific Subpopulation: Least Concern.

facts.claim: Coral Reefs in Kenya are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems as of 2024, having experienced a 30% coral cover loss over 50 years; CORDIO East Africa completed the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems of coral reefs for the Western Indian Ocean region in 2021.

sources.name: CORDIO East Africa
sources.quote: Current IUCN Red List of Ecosystems Status: Endangered (as of 2024). Population Trend: 30% coral cover loss over 50 years. CORDIO East Africa completed the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems of coral reefs of the Western Indian Ocean region in 2021.

facts.claim: US Passport Holders require an Electronic Travel Authorization (eTA) for Kenya before departure, as there is no visa on arrival. The eTA can be obtained online, is typically valid for a single entry for up to 90 consecutive days, or a multiple-entry eTA valid for five years is available. Travelers must carry a printout of the approved eTA, proof of accommodation, and a return or onward ticket. Passports must typically be valid for at least six months beyond the intended date of entry and have at least two blank pages.

facts.claim: For luxury lodges in the Maasai Mara, the most common and efficient transfer method from Jomo Kenyatta International Airport (NBO) is by scheduled or private charter flight from Nairobi's Wilson Airport (WIL) to various airstrips within or near the Maasai Mara National Reserve, followed by lodge vehicle transfers.

sources.name: Friendly Planet Travel
sources.quote: For luxury lodges in the Maasai Mara, the most common and efficient transfer method is by scheduled or private charter flight from Nairobi's Wilson Airport (WIL), a major domestic hub for safari flights. These flights land at various airstrips within or near the Maasai Mara National Reserve, from where lodge vehicles provide transfers.
